word_list.word: a horse of a different color

senses.definition: a completely different matter in comparison to what one used to deal with

examples.x: I thought I was going to a boring lecture, but the professor's teaching style was a horse of a different color.
The politician's usual speeches are full of empty promises, but his latest address was a horse of a different color.

senses.definition: an obvious issue, problem, or matter that people purposely ignore

examples.x: The student's plagiarism was the elephant in the room in the classroom, but the teacher didn't want to embarrass the student in front of the class.
The family's unspoken tension was the elephant in the room during the holidays, but no one wanted to ruin the festive mood by bringing it up.
The company's financial problems were the elephant in the room at the meeting, but no one wanted to talk about it.
The elephant in the room in the relationship was the issue of trust, but neither partner wanted to have an honest conversation about it.

senses.definition: an issue or subject that is not in any way connected to what one was talking about

examples.x: Managing a large team of employees is a different kettle of fish from managing a small team.
Raising a teenager is a different kettle of fish from raising a young child.

senses.definition: a different matter that requires one's consideration in order to be resolved

examples.x: Dealing with a customer complaint is a different cup of tea from handling a sales inquiry.
Parenting a child with special needs presents a unique set of challenges, making it a different cup of tea from raising a neurotypical child.
Resolving a conflict between two colleagues requires tact and diplomacy, a different cup of tea from addressing a technical issue.

senses.definition: (of a point or statement) to be applicable or relevant to both sides of an argument

examples.x: The argument that the government should not regulate businesses cuts both ways.
The claim that immigrants take jobs away from native-born workers cuts both ways.
The argument that social media is harmful to young people cuts both ways.
The claim that gun control laws are ineffective cuts both ways.
The argument that free trade is always beneficial cuts both ways.

senses.definition: a person who pretends to disagree with an opinion or idea just to promote a discussion concerning a particular subject

examples.x: I know everyone thinks we should support the new zoning proposal, but I'm going to play devil's advocate and say that I think it will have some negative consequences.
The teacher asked the students to play devil's advocate and argue against the author's thesis statement.
The politician was accused of being a flip-flopper, but he defended himself by saying that he was just playing devil's advocate in order to stimulate debate.
The lawyer played devil's advocate during the cross-examination of the witness, trying to poke holes in their testimony.
The reviewer played devil's advocate in their assessment of the film, pointing out its flaws even though they overall enjoyed it.

word_list.pronunciation: ɪt mˌeɪks nˈoʊ ɔːɹ lˈɪɾəl ˈɑːdz

senses.definition: used to state one's indifference to the way a situation develops or what its result will be

examples.x: He can take the bus or the train, it makes no odds to him as long as he gets there on time.
It makes little odds how much you practice, if you don't have natural talent, you won't succeed.
Whether you agree or not, it makes little odds to the outcome of the decision.
Whether you take the scenic route or the highway, it makes no odds to the length of the journey.

senses.definition: a reasoning or explanation that is deemed sufficient to justify or defend someone's actions

examples.x: The company's claims that the product is safe have no leg to stand on in light of the recent recall.
The politician's attempt to deny the allegations against him had no leg to stand on.
The accused's alibi was weak and lacked a leg to stand on.
The politician's denials were unconvincing and had no leg to stand on against the mounting evidence.
The team's excuses for its poor performance were lame and had no leg to stand on.

senses.definition: used to refer to a topic that is being talked about by a lot of people

examples.x: The news of the celebrity's divorce spread like wildfire, and within hours, it was on everybody's lips.
The new movie had been generating a lot of buzz in the weeks leading up to its release, and by the time it finally hit theaters, it was on everyone's lips.
The new fashion trend had taken the world by storm, and it was on everybody's lips.
